Mental Health Matters at Kaiser Permanente Northern California

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
The Permanente Medical Group, Inc.

21,782 followers

4w
  • Report this post

Mental health is health. 💙 This Mental Health Awareness Month, we're proud to shine a spotlight on the vital role mental health plays in overall health and well-being — and to celebrate our dedicated mental health care physicians and professionals who deliver high-quality, evidence-based care to our patients every day. Their work is making a difference: For seven consecutive years, Kaiser Permanente Northern California has been designated a best-rated health plan for behavioral and mental health care by California's Center for Data Insights and Innovation. This also marks the seventh year in a row that our California plans have been the only 5-out-of-5-star ranking recipients in the state. ⭐ Behind that recognition is real investment and growth: Kaiser Permanente has invested more than $2 billion since 2020 to expand mental health facilities and train and license new clinicians across the organization, and increased our mental health workforce in Northern California by 25%. And through our Kaiser Permanente Northern California Mental Health Training Collaborative, the largest non-governmental mental health training program in the U.S., we're training 600 aspiring therapists and psychologists this year alone. To all our mental health care providers: Thank you for showing up for patients, families, and members across Northern California. Your commitment to compassionate whole-person care is at the heart of what we do. 🙏 🔗 Learn about our mental health and wellness resources: https://lnkd.in/esh8rVbz   #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth

    How can a bold mental health campaign cut through and make a real impact? At LGcomms Academy 2026, Shokat Lal, Chief Executive of Sandwell Council, will be joined by Rebecca Hodgetts-Still and Emily Rees-Woodhall public health communications professionals at Sandwell Council, for the breakout session: ‘You ok mate?’ Taking place on Tuesday 23 June, the session will share learning from Sandwell Council’s award-nominated and hugely impactful mental health campaign, exploring how creative communications, community insight and behavioural science helped the campaign connect with people in meaningful ways. Shokat brings 36 years of senior experience across local government and health. Since joining Sandwell Council during a period of government intervention, he led the organisation’s rapid and successful exit, making Sandwell the fastest council to recover. He has also held senior roles at Rotherham and Barnsley and serves as a Ministerial Envoy to Tower Hamlets. Rebecca has worked at Sandwell Council for more than 20 years across housing, neighbourhoods, communications and public health. Working in marketing and behavioural science, she is passionate about campaigns that break away from the norm and help improve health outcomes for residents. Emily has more than five years’ experience in public health, designing and delivering campaigns across Sandwell. Her work combines community engagement with creative communication approaches to reach diverse audiences and support healthier lifestyles, including smoking cessation and mental health. Join us in Nottingham as LGcomms Academy 2026 helps public sector communicators Gear Up for the road ahead. Book here: https://lnkd.in/eD7jZ63b

      🟩 Today’s Mental Health Awareness Month IMHR member spotlight focuses on public health leader Jerome Adams, MD, MPH. 🥼 From serving as Indiana State Health Commissioner to becoming the 20th U.S. Surgeon General, Dr. Adams has consistently elevated the conversation around mental health, substance use, suicide prevention, and the powerful role that social determinants of health play in shaping both mental and physical wellbeing for Hoosiers and all Americans. 🎤 In February this year, Dr. Adams delivered the keynote for the 2026 Dr. Reaner and Mr. Henry Shannon Endowed Lectureship in Minority Health at the UMKC School of Medicine, speaking candidly about the state of mental health in America. Among the issues he addressed, Dr. Adams highlighted the urgent need to 𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗹𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗵 𝘀𝘂𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗱𝗲 — noting that suicide rates among younger age groups continue to rise, with a disproportionate impact on Black children ages 5–12, who are 𝘁𝘄𝗼 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲𝘀 𝗺𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗹𝗶𝗸𝗲𝗹𝘆 to die by suicide than their peers. This call builds on his leadership in Washington, where Dr. Adams released the landmark 𝘚𝘶𝘳𝘨𝘦𝘰𝘯 𝘎𝘦𝘯𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘭’𝘴 𝘊𝘢𝘭𝘭 𝘵𝘰 𝘈𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘐𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘕𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘭 𝘚𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘨𝘺 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘚𝘶𝘪𝘤𝘪𝘥𝘦 𝘗𝘳𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯, which continues to guide communities, health systems, educators, employers, and policymakers working to save lives and strengthen mental wellbeing. 🔬 Today, he continues that work as Executive Director of the Center for Community Health Empowerment and Learning at Purdue University (HEAL), where he leads cross-disciplinary efforts focused on reducing health disparities and improving health outcomes across Indiana and beyond. 💚 We are grateful for Dr. Adams’ continued leadership, his willingness to confront difficult but important conversations with clarity and compassion, and his commitment to helping communities across Indiana and the nation thrive. 🔗 Learn more about HEAL: https://lnkd.in/g-SfMT33 📹 Watch Dr. Adams’ UMKC lecture: https://lnkd.in/g2fFncDU

    • Golee Abrishami, PhD
      4w Edited

      Happy Mental Health Provider Appreciation Day! 🙌 I’ve spent nearly two decades in clinical practice, and I can still feel the weight of those early years. It’s a role that taught me early on that the foundation of a lasting bond isn’t just clinical insight, it’s the act of showing up consistently, even when things get hard. Being a clinician requires us to be many things at once: a skilled diagnostician, a compassionate witness, and a steady guide. This work requires us to navigate the unpredictable landscape of healing in real-time. This commitment to excellence is what builds a strong therapeutic alliance. That bond is a result of the time, effort and dedication a clinician has with their clients. It is the consistent work of showing up as a reliable support system to someone’s highest highs and lowest lows. At Octave, we see the impact of this dedication every day, with 89% of our clients citing this alliance as the primary foundation of their care. Being an impactful clinician is a balancing act of academic knowledge and human empathy. It is demanding, rewarding work that relies on the strength of both the client and the clinician. By focusing on the clinician’s skill and presence, we ensure that our clients' mental health journeys are rooted in a relationship that is deeply, authentically human. On Mental Health Provider Appreciation Day, I want to look directly at the team here at Octave. I am consistently blown away by the caliber of care this group provides. Our clinicians aren’t just showing up, they are actively redefining what high-quality mental health care looks like.  The trust a clinician builds through consistently showing up is what allows for real change. It’ s no coincidence that our clients experience 20-40% more improvement in anxiety and depression symptoms than the industry average. I am incredibly proud to work alongside a team that turns rigorous practice into such profound human impact. To all of the clinicians at Octave: Thank you for your skill, your heart, and your tireless commitment to this profession. Your expertise deserves recognition today and every day. ♥️ I am so proud to be in this work with you. #MentalHealthProviderAppreciationDay #ClinicalExcellence #Octave #TherapistAppreciation #MentalHealthCare

      This Mental Health Awareness Week, we want to highlight the important connection between digital inclusion and mental health support. 💚 Since COVID-19, online therapy and digital mental health services have become increasingly common. In 2023–2024 alone, 1.8 million people were referred for NHS talking therapies via phone, Zoom or Teams. But accessing support online is not always simple. It requires more than just a device; people also need internet access, digital skills, confidence using technology, and a safe, private space to engage with support services. For many young people, online spaces are already part of daily life, with 98% accessing at least one online community every day. Digital access can provide connection, support, and vital mental health resources when they are needed most. At the same time, 92% of people who apply for a laptop through our services say they are likely to use it to access online support services. This shows just how important digital access has become for wellbeing, healthcare, and staying connected. This Mental Health Awareness Week, we’re recognising that digital inclusion is about more than technology... It’s about ensuring everyone has equal access to support, connection, and opportunities to thrive. 💻✨ #MentalHeathAwarenessWeek #MentalHealthAwarenessWeek2026 #DigitalInclusion #MentalHealthSupport #DigitalAccess #CommunitySupport

      May marks #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth. As a country, we’ve moved the conversation forward. As a psychiatrist and clinician executive, I have discussions about the future of mental health every day. But the conversations I’m most pleased to hear come at the grocery store, at the soccer field, and in the course of everyday life. We’re in this together. Today, 1 in 5 adults in the U.S. experience some type mental illness. Yet too many people still face barriers to care driven by growing social isolation, persistent stigma and shortage of behavioral health providers. Awareness and comfort having these conversations will always be important. Our job now is to take action to address those barriers directly. It means designing care that is more accessible, more connected and easier to navigate at every step. At CVS Health, we’re focused on building a more integrated approach to mental health care, meeting people where they are through in-person, virtual and community-based support. Through CVS MinuteClinic, Oak Street Health, part of CVS Health, and other services, we’re expanding access to behavioral health and well-being resources across settings. Learn more at https: //https://lnkd.in/gRQNkB8j

    • Samantha Wood (FCMI)
      1w
      • Report this post

      Today at the Design in Mental Health Conference, I had the privilege of chairing two sessions in the Keynote Theatre, both focused on the continued development of Mental Health Emergency Departments (EDs). The first session explored the experience of a trust that has successfully developed and implemented a Mental Health ED, sharing valuable lessons learned and highlighting the elements that have contributed to positive outcomes. It was encouraging to hear practical insights from those leading innovation in this space. The second session brought a powerful lived experience perspective, exploring the complexities and tensions involved in designing Mental Health EDs that meet diverse needs. The discussion highlighted the challenge of balancing safety, functionality and comfort, while creating environments that promote dignity, recovery and person-centred care. Beyond the keynote sessions, it was inspiring to explore the many advancements taking place across the UK's mental health estate. There is a growing recognition that safety and therapeutic design are not competing priorities; when done well, they can work together to create environments that support both dignity and wellbeing. A huge thank you to all the speakers, delegates and exhibitors who continue to push the conversation forward. The future of mental health environments is being shaped by collaboration, innovation and, importantly, the voices of those with lived experience. #DesignInMentalHealth #MentalHealth #HealthcareDesign #MentalHealthED #LivedExperience #HealthcareInnovation #MentalHealthEstate

      💚 This Mental Health Awareness Month, we’re proud to spotlight the work of our Vice President of Medical Affairs, Dr. John (“Jack”) Westfall, and his continued leadership in advancing research focused on mental health equity and vulnerable populations. Dr. Westfall recently presented research at the NAPCRG examining the economic burden of mental health inequities among unhoused and incarcerated populations. The work highlights the devastating human and societal impacts of underdiagnosed and undertreated mental illness and substance use disorders, particularly among communities that already face significant barriers to care. 📊 The research estimates that inequities in mental health and substance use treatment contribute tens of billions of dollars in excess annual costs, while also leading to preventable suffering, poorer health outcomes, and premature loss of life. Importantly, the work emphasizes that adequate treatment and access to care could dramatically improve outcomes while reducing long-term societal costs. 📖 Dr. Westfall also authored Chapter 6, “The Economic Burden of Mental Health Inequities in the United States,” featured in: Mental Health Equity (Springer Publishing) At DARTNet, we’re grateful for Dr. Westfall’s leadership and his commitment to using research, collaboration, and evidence-based solutions to improve mental health outcomes and advance health equity nationwide. 🌎 #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #MentalHealthEquity #HealthEquity #PublicHealth #PrimaryCareResearch #BehavioralHealth #MentalHealthAwareness #DARTNet

      A strong mental health system depends on collaboration, this project with MHPN set out to show exactly how that happens. >>> We recently partnered with the Mental Health Professionals' Network (MHPN) to produce a brand video highlighting their role in supporting multidisciplinary, person-centred mental health care across Australia. MHPN plays an important role in the sector through CPD education, professional development webinars, and its national network program that connects mental health practitioners across disciplines. This video features the MHPN CEO alongside four partner representatives, each sharing perspectives on collaboration and the impact of coordinated care within their services. The objective was to clearly communicate MHPN’s value to funders and potential partners, showing how their work strengthens capability across the mental health system and supports better outcomes for patients. #MentalHealth #HealthcareVideo #HealthcareCommunications #VideoProduction #CorporateVideo #NotForProfit #MentalHealthAustralia #CPD #ProfessionalDevelopment #HealthSector #MultidisciplinaryCare #BrandVideo #VideoMarketing #PublicHealth #HealthcareStorytelling
